text: The patient is positioned in the lithotomy position with a slight Trendelenburg tilt, preparing for the surgical procedure.
The surgeon reproduces the rectal prolapse by grasping the apex of the prolapsed rectal tissue.
The surgeon resects the prolapsed rectum longitudinally at the three o'clock position using a reloadable 100mm GIA linear stapler, visibly excising the prolapsed tissue.
The surgeon waits for at least one minute before firing the stapler, allowing for the thickness and edema of the rectal tissues to settle before proceeding with the stapling.
The surgeon repeats the resection of the rectal prolapse at the nine o'clock position using a reloadable 100mm GIA linear stapler.
The surgeon has created anterior and posterior prolapsed rectal flaps, visible as distinct tissue sections during the procedure.
The surgeon resects the anterior rectal flap horizontally at the anorectal junction, carefully completing the resection parallel to and avoiding the dentate line.
The surgeon uses a reloadable endo-GIA linear stapling device, which is flexible and can be angled, to resect the posterior prolapsed rectal flap.
The surgeon uses a contour stapler for precise transection, resulting in the anal mucosa and neorectum spontaneously falling back into place after the complete resection.
